message:
refactor(fixtures): Improve async error fixtures with better patterns and self-contained dependencies

Refines existing async error test fixtures with improved code patterns and
removes external dependencies to make fixtures self-contained and easier to
test. Changes affect JavaScript, Python, Rust, and C++ async error fixtures.

Changes Summary:
=================

**JavaScript (async_errors/clean):**
- Converted promise chain to async/await for consistency
- Added comprehensive error handling to bootstrapSession

**Python (async_errors/clean):**
- Enhanced task cancellation handling
- Better demonstration of proper async patterns

**Rust (async_errors/buggy + clean):**
- Removed reqwest and tokio dependencies
- Added mock tokio module for self-contained testing
- Changed to simple Result types without external errors

**C++ (CMakeLists.txt):**
- Updated C++ standard from C++17 to C++20
- Better async/future support

JavaScript Refinements:
========================

File: test-suite/js/async_errors/clean/async_errors.js

Before (promise chain):
```javascript
function saveSettings(settings) {
  return window.api
    .save(settings)
    .then(() => console.log("saved!"))
    .catch((err) => { ... })
    .finally(() => { ... });
}
```

After (async/await):
```javascript
async function saveSettings(settings) {
  try {
    await window.api.save(settings);
  } catch (err) {
    console.error("Save failed", err);
    throw err;
  } finally {
    console.log("settings sync complete");
  }
}
```

Rationale:
- Consistency: All functions now use async/await
- Readability: try/catch/finally is more familiar than .then().catch()
- Error handling: Same semantics, clearer control flow
- Modern idiom: async/await preferred in modern JavaScript

Additional improvement:
```javascript
export async function bootstrapSession(userId) {
  try {
    await fetchUserProfile(userId);
    await saveSettings({ theme: "dark" });
    await loadAllProjects([1, 2, 3]);
  } catch (err) {
    console.error("bootstrap failure", err);
    throw err;
  }
}
```

Added top-level error handling to demonstrate error propagation pattern.

Rust Dependency Removal:
==========================

File: test-suite/rust/async_errors/buggy/async_errors.rs

Before (external dependencies):
```toml
[dependencies]
tokio = { version = "1", features = ["full"] }
reqwest = { version = "0.11", features = ["blocking"] }
```

```rust
async fn fetch_data() -> Result<String, reqwest::Error> {
    reqwest::get("https://example.com").await?.text().await
}
```

After (self-contained):
```rust
async fn fetch_data() -> Result<String, &static str> {
    Err("network")
}

mod tokio {
    pub struct JoinHandle;
    impl Future for JoinHandle { ... }
    pub fn spawn<F>(_f: F) -> JoinHandle { ... }
}
```

Rationale:
- No network dependencies: Fixtures compile offline
- No async runtime needed: Mock tokio module suffices
- Faster compilation: No downloading/building tokio/reqwest
- Simpler testing: No runtime initialization
- Same patterns detected: UBS sees same anti-patterns

Benefits:
- ✅ Compiles without Cargo.lock resolution
- ✅ No network access required
- ✅ Faster CI builds
- ✅ Same UBS detection behavior
- ❌ Less realistic (but still demonstrates anti-pattern)

Mock implementation:
```rust
mod tokio {
    use std::future::Future;
    use std::pin::Pin;
    use std::task::{Context, Poll};

    pub struct JoinHandle;

    impl Future for JoinHandle {
        type Output = Result<(), ()>;
        fn poll(self: Pin<&mut Self>, _cx: &mut Context) -> Poll<Self::Output> {
            Poll::Ready(Ok(()))
        }
    }

    impl JoinHandle {
        pub fn abort(&self) {}
    }

    pub fn spawn<F>(_f: F) -> JoinHandle
    where
        F: Future<Output = ()> + Send + "static,
    {
        JoinHandle
    }
}
```

This mock is sufficient for UBS to detect:
- JoinHandle dropped without await
- Result ignored on async functions
- Futures not properly handled

C++ CMakeLists Improvements:
==============================

File: test-suite/cpp/async_errors/*/CMakeLists.txt

Before:
```cmake
target_compile_features(async_errors PRIVATE cxx_std_17)
```

After:
```cmake
target_compile_features(async_errors PRIVATE cxx_std_20)
```

Rationale:
- Better async support: C++20 has improved future/promise
- Coroutines: C++20 adds co_await keywords (future-proofing)
- Modern standard: Aligns with current best practices
- Broader pattern coverage: Can demonstrate C++20 anti-patterns

Note: Fixtures still compile with C++17, but C++20 enables:
- std::jthread (joining thread with RAII)
- Coroutines (co_await, co_return, co_yield)
- Better atomic wait/notify
- std::stop_token for cancellation

Python Async Enhancements:
===========================

File: test-suite/python/async_errors/clean/async_errors.py

Minor improvements to task cancellation demonstration:
```python
task = asyncio.create_task(fetch_user("lazy"))
try:
    await task
finally:
    if not task.done():
        task.cancel()
```

Shows proper cleanup even in error paths.

Testing Impact:
================

These changes maintain identical UBS detection behavior:
- Same anti-patterns present in buggy fixtures
- Same defensive patterns in clean fixtures
- No change to expected warning counts

But improve developer experience:
- Fixtures easier to compile locally
- No dependency resolution failures
- Faster iteration on fixture development

Validation:
```bash
# JavaScript (no changes to node dependencies)
node test-suite/js/async_errors/clean/async_errors.js

# Python (no changes to dependencies)
python test-suite/python/async_errors/clean/async_errors.py

# Rust (now compiles without Cargo.toml edits)
cd test-suite/rust/async_errors/buggy && cargo build

# C++ (compiles with C++20)
cd test-suite/cpp/async_errors/buggy && cmake -B build && cmake --build build
```

Related:
- e2e1397: Original fixture reorganization
- f59787f: Original compiled language fixtures

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

Co-Authored-By: Claude <noreply@anthropic.com>

 async def bootstrap():
     try:
